title: Foundations: Skeptic-Safe On-Ramp and Disciplinary Anchor
description: Begin with disciplinary values, a functional understanding of generative AI, and a grounded stance that refuses both hype and panic.
artifact: Discipline Statement and signed Skeptic's Charter
objectives: Explain generative AI in functional, non-mystical terms.
Name a discipline-specific cognitive practice AI must not replace.
Create a values anchor for every later design decision.

videos.title: Your disciplinary line in the sand
videos.transcript: This lecture introduces grounded curiosity, the six portfolio artifacts, and the Skeptic's Charter.
videos.summary: This lecture asks you to name your disciplinary line in the sand: what AI must not damage in your teaching. It gives skepticism a legitimate place, warns against the two default postures (AI as all-knowing Oracle or as cheating Adversary to police), and introduces the bounded Tutor stance. The work product is a three-sentence Skeptic's Charter — what AI must not weaken, what evidence would show damage, and what responsible use would look like — which becomes the first section of your Discipline Statement.

videos.title: How LLMs Work for Educators
videos.transcript: A functional explanation of prediction, fluency, hallucination, bias, and overconfidence.
videos.summary: A plain-language working model of large language models for teaching decisions: an LLM is a probabilistic next-token predictor, so fluency is not evidence of understanding. The lecture shows why hallucination, bias, and overconfidence are properties of the mechanism rather than passing bugs, maps where these tools are strong (summarizing, translating, drafting structure) versus risky (citations, niche facts, sustained reasoning), and introduces the jagged, always-moving frontier of capability. The takeaway for grading: if fluent work can exist without understanding, assignments cannot rely on fluency as their evidence.

videos.title: How LLMs Work: A Technical Review
videos.transcript: A deeper technical review of how large language models work.
videos.summary: A deeper dive into the machinery behind the 'predict the next word' summary: parameters at billion-to-trillion scale, the transformer's self-attention mechanism, and the training pipeline from pre-training through supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback. It explains tokens, context windows, and temperature — why the same prompt can produce different answers — and why confident style is learned independently of factual accuracy (the '42 phenomenon'). The mental model to keep: autocomplete at planetary scale, impressive and unreliable for the same underlying reason.

videos.title: Risk is a design question
videos.transcript: Connect technical, applied, and critical AI literacy to disciplinary choices.
videos.summary: This lecture reframes AI risk as a design question rather than a panic button. It sizes risk with a three-part heuristic — capability, exposure, consequence — and pairs the main system limitations (hallucination, bias, data exposure) with proportionate classroom responses like named-source verification and a no-sensitive-uploads rule. It distinguishes intentional misuse from good-faith overreliance, which needs verification and independent capability checks rather than punishment, and ends by asking you to name one risk, one response, and the tradeoff that response creates for a single task.

videos.title: AI literacy is a disciplinary act
videos.transcript: Turn general AI literacy into a specific disciplinary commitment.
videos.summary: AI literacy is presented as a disciplinary act: a historian, a nurse, and a finance student do not need the same version of it. The lecture defines AI literacy as the capacity to understand, use, question, and govern AI in relation to human judgment, and offers three working tiers — technical, applied, and critical. The key design move is replacing the phrase 'critical thinking' with the specific cognitive practice your field requires, then drafting a Discipline Statement that protects both the discipline and access to it.

reading.title: Module 1 Reading — Foundations: Grounded Curiosity and Your Disciplinary Anchor
reading.description: Grounded curiosity, what an LLM actually does, Oracle/Adversary/Tutor, the three tiers of AI literacy, and the equity conditions that shape your Discipline Statement and Skeptic's Charter.

title: The Cognitive Architecture of Learning
description: Distinguish productive struggle from needless friction and choose an appropriate AI posture for a real learning task.
objectives: Identify where AI can widen the novice-expert divide.
Separate productive struggle from needless friction.
Choose an Oracle, Tutor, or Adversary posture deliberately.

videos.title: The Safety Gap and the Novice-Expert Divide
videos.transcript: Why experts and novices experience AI assistance differently.
videos.summary: This lecture names the Safety Gap: the distance between what an AI-assisted product appears to demonstrate and what a student can independently do. It explains why the same fluent output that extends an expert's thinking can short-circuit a novice's practice — novices lack the mental models needed to check and calibrate — and why usable understanding is built through retrieval and correction. The design response is a Cognitive Sanctuary: a bounded course moment that protects one essential act of student thinking and makes hidden capability visible without surveillance.

videos.title: Productive Struggle vs. Needless Friction
videos.transcript: A design lens for deciding what difficulty belongs in the learning task.
videos.summary: A working distinction between productive struggle (difficulty that builds learning, like retrieval, spacing, interleaving, and elaboration) and needless friction (difficulty that only blocks access, like fighting formats or decoding unrelated jargon). AI can remove both, so the design task is to protect the first and repair the second — the lecture calls the second move an Access Repair and shows why it raises rather than lowers standards. A useful test: would removing the difficulty also remove the evidence of learning? The equity stake is direct, because needless friction falls hardest on students already facing access barriers.

videos.title: Oracle, Tutor, Adversary
videos.transcript: Three postures for structuring AI use without outsourcing judgment.
videos.summary: Three postures AI can take in an assignment — Oracle (gives answers; fast but high-risk for novices), Tutor (questions and hints that keep the student retrieving and explaining), and Adversary (produces flawed output for the student to critique). The lecture shows the two common mismatches, novice-plus-Oracle (illusion of competence) and novice-plus-Adversary (shallow critique), and argues that every posture needs one short, observable boundary to keep it from collapsing back into answer-generation. You leave with one posture instruction, one boundary rule, and one sentence naming the thinking being protected.

reading.title: Module 2 Reading — Friction, Productive Struggle, and Learning Design
reading.description: The Safety Gap, desirable difficulties, productive struggle versus needless friction, and how to design one Cognitive Sanctuary and one Access Repair before the Friction Audit.

title: Assessment Redesign and the AI Assessment Scale
description: Stress-test an assessment, decide what it truly measures, and document a defensible AI Assessment Scale choice.
artifact: Assessment Stress Test and AIAS Decision Memo
objectives: Distinguish learning production from learning measurement.
Use the AI Assessment Scale as a design vocabulary.
Complete a failure analysis before redesigning an assignment.

videos.title: Learning Production vs. Learning Measurement
videos.transcript: Ask whether an assessment measures the intended learning or only the production of a deliverable.
videos.summary: Most assignments quietly do three jobs at once: production (practice that builds skill), measurement (evidence that certifies it), and logistics (course plumbing). This lecture shows why AI makes that ambiguity expensive — the highest-risk pattern is letting students practice with AI and then grading as if they practiced without it — and walks through annotating each sentence of a prompt by its job. Small relabeling moves in the spirit of TILT let you attach different AI expectations to different components and give differently prepared students a visible sequence of practice, support, and proof.

videos.title: The AI Assessment Scale (AIAS)
videos.transcript: Use AIAS levels to communicate permitted and expected AI involvement.
videos.summary: An introduction to the AI Assessment Scale (AIAS), five assessment contracts running from no AI, through AI-assisted planning and collaboration, to full AI use and AI-as-object-of-critique. Each level changes three things together — what the student does, what AI does, and what evidence is required — which is why the scale works best applied per component rather than pasted across a whole course. The lecture pairs the scale with an access plan (no-cost path, privacy rule, fallback) and distinguishes the assignment contract from the integrity policy that backs it.

videos.title: The Stress Test is a failure analysis
videos.transcript: Identify where AI can bypass essential cognitive work before choosing a response.
videos.summary: The Stress Test treats your assignment as the object of a failure analysis before students arrive: what can a current AI tool produce, what learning sits invisibly below the polished product, what small piece of process evidence would restore confidence, and what equity cost would that fix create? Results feed a decision tree — keep, revise, split, replace, or integrate AI — with detect-and-punish deliberately absent. A risk heat map focuses limited redesign time where bypass is likely and the learning consequence is high, and the output is a short Decision Memo a colleague could challenge.

reading.title: Module 3 Reading — Assessment Stress Testing and the AI Assessment Scale
reading.description: Learning production versus learning measurement, the five AIAS levels as contracts, the four-question Stress Test, and a Decision Memo that answers its strongest counterargument.

title: Assignment Redesign: From AI-Proofing to AI-Resilience
description: Redesign a real assignment so the essential thinking is visible, supported, and difficult to bypass without creating unsustainable work.
objectives: Make student thinking visible without making workload explode.

videos.title: AI-proof is the Wrong Goal
videos.transcript: Why durable design begins with the learning rather than an arms race against tools.
videos.summary: AI-proofing an assignment enters an arms race it cannot win: capabilities drift, false confidence sets in, enforcement lands unevenly, and task design turns brittle. This lecture replaces that goal with AI-resilience — an assignment whose evidence of learning stays meaningful whether or not students use AI — built through three strategies: process visibility, use clarity, and authentic context. The redesign triangle keeps the learning goal, the AIAS level, and the evidence form pointing at the same outcome, with the lock-versus-blueprint contrast as the mindset shift.

videos.title: Making Thinking Visible Without Making Work Explode
videos.transcript: Choose lightweight process evidence that supports judgment and feedback.
videos.summary: How to make student thinking visible without drowning anyone in paperwork. The one-sentence test — what learning decision does this element reveal? — separates evidence from compliance, and a workload audit (about thirty minutes of student time and ten minutes of review per submission as warning thresholds) keeps the element honest at real enrollments. The rule of one says add at most one process-evidence element per assignment, and the lecture warns against the surveillance trap of demanding complete prompt logs, offering selected excerpts plus a brief reflection instead.

videos.title: Using AI as Tutor, Adversary, or Collaborator
videos.transcript: Build AI into a task only where its role strengthens the intended learning.
videos.summary: When AI is integrated on purpose, vague permission defaults students into Oracle use. This lecture specifies three designed postures — Tutor (questions before answers, hints after documented attempts), Adversary (flawed output the student critiques; the critique is the submission), and Collaborator (student drafts first, AI suggests, student documents accept/reject decisions) — each matched to AIAS levels and protected by a boundary with a trigger, a documentation response, and a restorative follow-up. A free-tier check confirms the assignment works without paid access before it ships.

reading.title: Module 4 Reading — Redesigning Assignments with TILT and AI-Aware Scaffolding
reading.description: AI-resilience instead of AI-proofing: the TILT rewrite, the AI Use box, exactly one process-evidence element, and posture-preserving boundaries for Tutor, Adversary, and Collaborator.

title: Transparent Policies, Integrity, and the Equity/Ethics Audit
description: Turn assignment decisions into transparent course policy, replace detection-led thinking, and audit implementation for equity and ethics.
artifact: Syllabus AI Policy and Equity/Ethics Audit
objectives: Explain why detection cannot serve as standalone evidence.
Align course policy with assignment-level AI choices.
Audit policy across access, privacy, truth, copyright, labor, and power.

videos.title: Why Detection Fails as a Teaching Strategy
videos.transcript: Replace unreliable detection with a designed evidence stack.
videos.summary: Why detection cannot carry an integrity process: a detector score is a probabilistic signal with unstable error rates, documented false-positive bias against non-native English writers, and a chilling effect on the help-seeking that integrity actually depends on. The lecture replaces it with an evidence stack you design — process evidence in the assignment, a low-stakes conversation, comparison to demonstrated capability, and required documentation — ordered restoratively: notice, converse, then respond according to what the evidence shows. Policy language shifts from deterrence by threat to definition and invitation.

videos.title: Core Intellectual Substance and Student-Facing Policy
videos.transcript: Write policy that names what students must do and why.
videos.summary: The center of a defensible policy is core intellectual substance: the thinking, judgment, and final claims students must own in your course. The lecture shows why blanket bans, anything-goes permissiveness, and vague 'use AI responsibly' language all fail students, then builds a 150-to-250-word policy from seven components, including documentation expectations, a privacy warning, and an explicit help-seeking invitation. A read-aloud test checks the tone, a decision tree gives students a safe next action at every point (including after a mistake), and the policy must agree with each assignment's stated AIAS level.

videos.title: The Equity and Ethics Audit
videos.transcript: Treat equity and ethics as design constraints rather than closing disclaimers.
videos.summary: An audit across eight dimensions — access, language and disability, privacy, truth and verification, copyright, labor and environment, bias, and detection — with one standard throughout: ethics is a design constraint, not a disclaimer. Each dimension comes with operational checks, like requiring a no-cost equivalent path, permitting translation when language production is not the outcome, treating AI output as never-a-source, and declining to authorize detection. The audit's discipline is that it must change one concrete sentence in your policy or assignment before the policy is finalized.

reading.title: Module 5 Reading — Syllabus AI Policy, Equity, Ethics, and Course Norms
reading.description: Why detection fails as a teaching strategy, core intellectual substance, the seven-component policy paragraph, and the eight-dimension equity and ethics audit.

title: Portfolio, Departmental Alignment, and Continuous Revision
description: Assemble a teaching-ready packet, identify department-level alignment needs, and establish a sustainable capability-drift review cadence.
artifact: Final Course Redesign Portfolio and Six-Month Plan
objectives: Identify shared language without forcing uniformity.
Create a sustainable six-month maintenance plan.

videos.title: Assembling a Portfolio Faculty
videos.transcript: Build a teaching-ready packet rather than a reflective essay about AI.
videos.summary: Assembly time: the portfolio separates a student-facing layer (redesigned assignment, syllabus AI policy, first-week touchpoint) from an instructor-facing layer (your Discipline Statement, audits, Stress Test, and six-month plan). The quality bar is the substitute-instructor test — could a capable colleague teach from this packet without calling you? — supported by a 100-word cover memo and four readiness checks: syllabus-ready, LMS-ready, grading-ready, student-ready. The one reflective act asked of you is rereading your Module One Skeptic's Charter and writing a paragraph on what the course honored, challenged, and left standing.

videos.title: Departmental Alignment Without Forced Uniformity
videos.transcript: Align shared vocabulary and equity floors while preserving course-level judgment.
videos.summary: One student, three courses, three contradictory AI rules: this lecture treats unexplained contradiction (not difference) as the integrity failure. The resolution is a middle path — departments align on shared AIAS vocabulary, a minimum equity floor, and a documented integrity process, while each course keeps the level, posture, and evidence-form decisions that depend on its learning goals. A five-scenario anonymous poll surfaces where colleagues quietly disagree, and your move is small: one department-level question worth raising, one course-level decision you can make now.

videos.title: Designing for Capability Drift
videos.transcript: Identify what becomes stale first and budget a sustainable review cadence.
videos.summary: Capability drift outpaces syllabus revision, so the answer is a small scheduled maintenance habit rather than perpetual redesign. Triage your materials into high-drift (tool names, capability claims, detection assumptions), moderate-drift (local vocabulary, contract-tied policy), and low-drift (the frameworks themselves); revisit when two trigger events occur, with an immediate override for policy, privacy, or accessibility changes. Vintage stamps on every capability claim make aging visible, and the budget is deliberately modest: one assignment, one Stress Test question, actions of thirty minutes, about ninety minutes per semester.

reading.title: Module 6 Reading — Portfolio Integration, Maintenance, and the Six-Month Plan
reading.description: The teaching-ready portfolio, the substitute-instructor test, reopening the Skeptic's Charter, capability-drift triage, and a six-month plan that fits in 90 minutes a semester.
